tickets.title: publishRegistration and a process-local override — should a durable binding replace one? (diagnosis landed; swallow rejected)
tickets.body:
PART (1) LANDED: the did-not-bind diagnosis no longer guesses. The fixpoint still swallows a candidate's failure (one bad registration must not fail a boot) but REMEMBERS it, and publishRegistration reports the proximate cause verbatim.

THE SWALLOW WAS TRIED AND REJECTED (2026-07-20, under review). The idea: publishing law that a process-local `register()` already holds is an OVERRIDE, not a failure - the deltas persist and would bind on a later boot - so publish should return quietly instead of throwing. Implemented, reviewed, reverted. Three reasons, all fatal:
  (a) "same law" cannot be established. `registerImpl` takes NO resolvers argument, so a manual binding never carries any; a publish shipping resolvers (or mutations, or different roots, or a different Schema) matched the narrow name+body test and was swallowed as identical - leaving a green publish over a surface that implements none of it, silently.
  (b) the check read the FLAT bound list, which legitimately holds superseded manual bindings, so a binding serving nothing could authorize the swallow - the exact hazard `programReadings` documents for itself.
  (c) it discarded the remembered reason without establishing that shadowing CAUSED the failure; publish-time and replay-time validation differ on ungoverned stores (`operatorAuthor ?? authorForSeed(seed)` vs `?? "loam:specimen"`), so a template failure could be reported as a benign override.
A true sentence beats a quiet wrong surface. The throw stays.

WHAT REMAINS - a decision (Myk's): should a durable published binding REPLACE a process-local one rather than be shadowed by it? `replayRegistrationsImpl` seeds `accepted` with every `origin: "manual"` binding, so the manual one wins and the durable one is left unbound. Arguably backwards - the form that survives a reboot might outrank the one that does not - but a process pinning its own surface in code is a real counter-argument. Whatever is chosen should be STATED in SS21; today it is emergent. Note also that `register()` cannot evolve a lens in place at all (it throws on the GraphQL field collision, appending without dropping the superseded binding).
